title: Triund Trek
summary: 2D/1N from Bhagsunag, Dharamshala — waterfall trail, Dhauladhar views, and camping under the stars. From ₹999.
overview:
The Triund trek in the Himalayas is a relatively easy and popular trail, offering stunning views of the snow-covered Dhauladhar ranges and Kangra Valley. It is an excellent option for anyone looking for a hassle-free weekend trek in the region. The trail’s end has a steep climb, but the overall route is short.

Triund is one of the most popular treks in India. The vast expanse near Triund Top boasts lush green meadows with deodar, oak, pine, and rhododendron forests — ideal for nature lovers. Whether you prefer a warm summer outing or a snowy winter walk, the trek is open through much of the year, except during the heavy monsoon season.

Join us for an adventure to witness nature at its finest. Book the Triund trek with friends or family this weekend and experience the magic of the Dhauladhars.

itinerary.title: Bhagsunag to Karthani (via Bhagsu Waterfall)
itinerary.description:
The Bhagsunag Taxi Stand is the starting point. Please arrive by 10:00 AM. The trail passes through forests of deodar, oak, fir, and rhododendron, and goes via Bhagsu waterfall and Shiva café, with panoramic views of Bhagsu village, McLeod Ganj, Dharamshala, and Kangra Valley.

From the waterfall the path becomes steeper and rockier as you climb toward Triund. The trail is marked, and small tea stalls along the way are useful for lunch breaks and water refill. In about 3–4 hours you reach Karthani — roughly 700 meters below Triund Top — for overnight camping.

If weather and forest department rules allow, enjoy sunset colours on the Dhauladhars, a campfire session, and dinner before retiring to your tent.

itinerary.title: Karthani to Triund Top & return to Bhagsunag
itinerary.description:
After breakfast, hike about 1 km uphill from Karthani to Triund Top (around one hour) for sunrise views and time to explore the ridge meadows facing the Dhauladhar range.

Then descend roughly 8 km to Bhagsunag through forests and mountain landscapes, typically finishing by about 12:00 PM. Reflect on the journey as you return toward McLeod Ganj for onward travel.

Camping gear — tents, sleeping bags, sleeping mats, kitchen utensils

fitness.description: Triund suits beginners with basic fitness. Expect 3–5 hours of walking with a sustained uphill section. You should be comfortable on inclined trails with a light daypack.
tips: Walk 3–4 km daily for two weeks before departure
Practice stair climbs with a light backpack
Hydrate well and avoid alcohol the night before
Break in your trek shoes beforehand
Arrive at Bhagsunag by 10:00 AM on Day 1

Day 2 summits Triund Top (~2,850 m) then descends ~8 km back to Bhagsunag. Reach Dharamshala by air (Gaggal), overnight bus from Delhi/Chandigarh, or train to Pathankot plus a 2–3 hour road transfer.

faqs.question: Is the network available in Triund?
faqs.answer: During the trek, there are spots where the cell network is accessible. When you reach the top, the network often disappears. It is best to let your family know that you may not be reachable for some time.

faqs.question: What is the best way to book a Triund Trek online?
faqs.answer: You can book the Triund Trek package directly on this website. The standard camping package starts from ₹999 per person and covers camping, guide support, dinner, and breakfast as listed in the inclusions.

faqs.question: Is camping allowed in Triund?
faqs.answer: Night camping rules can change with forest department guidelines. When permitted, camping is arranged in designated zones to manage crowd and environmental impact. Your trek leader will brief you on the approved overnight location (typically Karthani / Triund zone).

faqs.question: Can Triund trek be done in one day?
faqs.answer: Yes, Triund can be completed as a single-day hike in about 4–6 hours. For sunrise, sunset, and a night under the stars, we recommend the 2 days / 1 night camping package.

faqs.question: Is Triund Trek safe for kids?
faqs.answer: Yes, families often do this trek, but the climb can tire younger children. Be prepared to help or carry kids on steeper stretches. Share your child’s age with us when booking so we can advise accordingly.

faqs.question: Is night stay allowed in Triund?
faqs.answer: Overnight camping in the Triund area is allowed as per current state guidelines when arranged through approved operators and designated camping zones. Availability depends on weather and permits on the day of the trek.

faqs.question: Can a beginner do Triund Trek?
faqs.answer: The route has sustained uphill walking and can feel tiring for absolute first-timers, but with an easy-to-moderate grade and guide support, beginners with basic fitness regularly complete it successfully.

faqs.question: How much does the Triund trek cost?
faqs.answer: Triund Trek with camping generally starts from ₹999 per person for triple sharing. Double sharing options are available at a higher rate. Exact package prices are shown on this page.

faqs.question: Which time is best for Triund Trek?
faqs.answer: The best months are May, June, September, and October. Weather is generally pleasant, though nights can be chilly. Monsoon is scenic but less ideal for overnight camping; winter can bring snow and extra cold.

faqs.question: Is Triund Trek safe for girls?
faqs.answer: Yes. Triund is a popular and well-trafficked trail, commonly done by solo travelers and women trekkers when booking with a certified guided group.

faqs.question: Is Triund trek difficult?
faqs.answer: It is rated easy to moderate. The trek is short but includes a steep final climb. Most fit beginners complete it comfortably with paced breaks.

faqs.question: How long is a Triund Trek?
faqs.answer: The round trip is around 16 km (both ways). A day hike typically takes 4–6 hours. With camping, plan for 2 days / 1 night to enjoy sunset, night sky, and sunrise at or near Triund Top.

faqs.question: What is included in the trek fee?
faqs.answer: Evening tea, dinner and breakfast (veg), experienced certified trekking guide, camping gear (tents, sleeping bags, mats, kitchen utensils), first-aid kit with the team, and pit-style toilet tents. See the full inclusions list on this page.

faqs.question: How do I reach the starting point?
faqs.answer: Reach Dharamshala / McLeod Ganj first. The trek starts from Bhagsunag Taxi Stand. By air: Gaggal Airport. By bus: overnight coaches from Delhi (about 8–10 hours) or Chandigarh. By train: Pathankot, then a 2–3 hour bus or cab to Dharamshala. Arrive by 10:00 AM on Day 1.

title: Kareri Lake Trek
summary: 2D/1N from Kareri Village near Dharamshala — oak forests, Nyund stream, and a high freshwater alpine lake. From ₹1,499.
overview:
Kareri Lake sits at the foothills of the Dhauladhar range in Kangra Valley, about 9 km above Kareri Village. It is a high-altitude freshwater lake fed by meltwater from snowy peaks, with clear shallows where you can often see the lake bed.

The approach winds through silver oak, rhododendron, and Chir pine beside the Nyund stream. Meadows around the lake double as seasonal grazing grounds for Gaddi shepherds before they cross Minkiani and Baleni passes toward Chamba and Bharmour.

From December to March the lake frequently freezes and access may need technical mountaineering gear. In open season it is an easy-to-moderate weekend trek for fit beginners who want quieter trails than the busiest Dharamshala day hikes.

itinerary.title: Kareri Village to Kareri Lake via Riyoti
itinerary.description:
Meet at Kareri Village by 10:00 AM (earlier arrival helps). The trail climbs through silver oak, rhododendron, and longleaf Chir pine, then steepens along the Nyund glacial stream toward the lake (~2,934–3,100 m).

Pause for rest and packed lunch at Riyoti (also called Reoti / Liyoti) — the halfway camp. Weather decides the final push to the lake; from December to March ice and deep snow can block casual access without mounts gear.

Overnight in dome tents at Kareri Lake camp under the Dhauladhars.

itinerary.title: Kareri Lake to Kareri Village
itinerary.description:
After breakfast, descend 3–4 hours to Kareri Village with the lake vistas still fresh. The trek ends here by about 2:00 PM.

If needed, your guide can help arrange a taxi back into Dharamshala or McLeod Ganj at an extra cost.

Carry a packed lunch for Day 1

faqs.question: Can we visit Kareri Lake in December?
faqs.answer: April to December generally works for organising the trek, covering summer, monsoon, and early winter moods. From late December through March the lake often freezes and may need technical gear — check conditions before you book a winter date.

faqs.question: Which trek is better — Triund or Kareri?
faqs.answer: Triund is busier and closer to McLeod Ganj, with classic Dhauladhar views. Kareri Lake is quieter and suits hikers who want a freshwater lake, forest trails, and fewer crowds. Many travellers do both on separate weekends.

faqs.question: Does it snow at Kareri Lake?
faqs.answer: Yes. At this altitude snow is common mid-December through February. Trails and the lake shore can be snowbound — winter visits need warmer gear and guide judgement on route safety.

faqs.question: Can Kareri Lake be reached by car?
faqs.answer: No. Drive or take a cab to Kareri Village (Kareri Khas) near Dharamshala, then begin the trek on foot. The lake itself is reached only by trail.

faqs.question: Is there network at Kareri Lake?
faqs.answer: Expect little or no coverage on the trail and at the lake. Even BSNL is intermittent. Carry cash — digital payments are unreliable once you leave the village.

faqs.question: How can I reach Kareri from Chandigarh?
faqs.answer: Buses and shared cabs run Chandigarh to Dharamshala (roughly 6–7 hours). From Dharamshala, hire a taxi to Kareri Village and start the trek by 10:00 AM on Day 1.

faqs.question: How do I go to Kareri Lake from Dharamshala?
faqs.answer: Hire a jeep or cab from Dharamshala toward Ghera / Kareri Village (about 20+ km by road depending on route). The trek starts from Kareri Village and follows the Nyund stream uphill to the lake.

faqs.question: Can I do the Kareri Lake trek alone?
faqs.answer: Solo travellers regularly join guided groups. Booking with a certified guide is safer for navigation, weather calls, and camping logistics.

faqs.question: How do you do the Kareri Trek?
faqs.answer: Reach Dharamshala first. Local buses go toward Ghera; from Ghera, Kareri Village is about a 2-hour walk, or take a cab direct to the village. Meet the group by 10:00 AM and trek via Riyoti to the lake camp.

faqs.question: How long is the trek from Kareri Village?
faqs.answer: One way is roughly 9–10 km (about 20 km both ways). Expect 4–5 hours uphill via Riyoti halfway camp, and 3–4 hours on the return.

faqs.question: Can you swim in Kareri Lake?
faqs.answer: It is a shallow freshwater lake. Some people swim in summer when the water is open; in winter the surface often freezes under deep snow on the approach — swimming is not advisable then.

faqs.question: Can we do Kareri Lake trek in one day?
faqs.answer: A same-day out-and-back is tough (roughly 20 km both ways with climb). Most people prefer 2 days / 1 night to enjoy the lakeside camp and avoid a rushed dark descent.

faqs.question: Is Kareri Lake Trek easy?
faqs.answer: It is easy to intermediate for anyone with reasonable fitness. Much of the path is straightforward, but some rocky uphill stretches demand paced walking and sturdy shoes.

faqs.question: What is included in the trek fee?
faqs.answer: Day 1 evening tea and dinner, Day 2 breakfast, certified guide, camping gear (tents, sleeping bags, mats, kitchen utensils), bonfire when available, and pit-style toilet tents. Forest or other permits are not included.

title: Indrahar Pass Trek
summary: Cross the iconic Indrahar Pass (≈4,342 m) on the Dhauladhar divide between Kangra and Chamba — serious altitude, scree, and huge Himalayan views. From ₹8,999.
overview:
Indrahar Pass Trek is part of India Holiday Destinations’ Dharamshala and Dhauladhar collection. Cross the iconic Indrahar Pass (≈4,342 m) on the Dhauladhar divide between Kangra and Chamba — serious altitude, scree, and huge Himalayan views.

Indrahar is the classic Kangra–Chamba gateway: scree, thin air, and a sudden reveal of Chamba’s layered ranges once you crest the notch. This is not a casual weekend extension of Triund — treat it as a proper high pass.

The itinerary usually starts at Bhagsunag / Dharamkot and ends at Chamba side or return via McLeod, spanning about 32 km across 4 day(s) with a high point near 14,250 ft. Graded challenging, it demands respect for Himachal weather, Leave No Trace camping, and honest self-assessment before you book.

Best time to visit: April–June and September–November for most stable Dhauladhar weather

Flora along the Indrahar Pass corridor: Regional mix of chir pine, ban oak, rhododendron, deodar, and high-altitude scrub depending on elevation band.

Wildlife notes for Indrahar Pass Trek: Himalayan birds (monal, griffons), langurs in forests, and occasional goral on steep grass — observe from distance.

Photography spots: Indrahar Pass main viewpoint; Valley light at golden hour; Camp ridgeline panoramas; Village / temple cultural frames.

Camping locations: Designated Indrahar Pass camping zones; Backup lower camp in bad weather.

Water sources: Carry adequate water from the last village; treat natural sources before drinking.

Network availability: Expect patchy or zero network beyond the last town. BSNL sometimes works in remote valleys.

Electricity availability: No reliable charging on trail — carry power banks.

ATM availability: Last reliable ATMs in Dharamshala, McLeod Ganj, Palampur, Baijnath, or Bharmour depending on approach.

Medical facilities: Basic trail first aid; nearest substantial care in Dharamshala / regional district hospitals.

Permits required: Inner forest / camping permits as required for the specific corridor; included when stated in package.

Forest permissions: Follow Himachal Forest Department and local administration rules for camping, fire, and waste.

Safety on Indrahar Pass: Stay on marked trails and follow your trek leader’s briefings. Turn back early if weather deteriorates — Dhauladhar storms build quickly. Never wander alone after dark near cliffs, snow patches, or stream crossings. Always follow your trek leader’s brief for this specific route.

Travel tips: Arrive in McLeod Ganj a night early when mornings start at the trailhead. Download offline maps; do not rely on trail network. Carry cash for village tea shops and emergency transport.

Responsible tourism on Indrahar Pass Trek: Leave no trace — pack out all non-biodegradable waste. Use designated camping zones and toilet practices advised by your guide. Support local porters, mules, and homestays fairly.

Nearby attractions from this trailhead: McLeod Ganj, Bhagsu Nag, Kangra Fort, Bir Billing, Masroor Temples.

Nearby treks: Kareri Village Trek, Minkiani Pass Trek, Guna Devi Trek, Snowline Laka Trek.

Child policy for Indrahar Pass Trek: 16+ experienced preferred. Children are welcome on easier Dhauladhar trails when accompanied by a parent or guardian. Minimum age depends on the route (typically 8+ for short day hikes, 12+ for overnight alpine camps). Share ages at booking for route-specific advice.

Private departures: Private Indrahar Pass Trek departures can be arranged for families and corporate groups subject to guide availability.

Group discount: Group discount available for 6+ travellers on the same fixed departure.

Booking and cancellation policies for India Holiday Destinations packages apply — see FAQs on this page for the current fee schedule.

Things to carry beyond the packing list: Layered clothing; Rain shell; Headlamp; Personal medicines; Reusable water bottle.

Things not to carry: Single-use plastics beyond reusable bottles; Heavy jeans or cotton-heavy clothing for multi-day wet weather; Unnecessary valuables and excess jewellery; Alcohol and recreational drugs.

title: Minkiani Pass Trek
summary: A demanding Dhauladhar crossing above Kareri Lake via Minkiani Pass — remote meadows, high camps, and few crowds. From ₹11,999.
overview:
Minkiani Pass Trek is part of India Holiday Destinations’ Dharamshala and Dhauladhar collection. A demanding Dhauladhar crossing above Kareri Lake via Minkiani Pass — remote meadows, high camps, and few crowds.

Minkiani sits above the Kareri basin as a lonelier sibling to Indrahar. Expect long meadow days, cold nights, and navigation that rewards patience more than speed.

The itinerary usually starts at Kareri Village and ends at Chamba / return via Kareri, spanning about 40 km across 5 day(s) with a high point near 13,900 ft. Graded challenging, it demands respect for Himachal weather, Leave No Trace camping, and honest self-assessment before you book.

Best time to visit: April–June and September–November for most stable Dhauladhar weather

Flora along the Minkiani Pass corridor: Regional mix of chir pine, ban oak, rhododendron, deodar, and high-altitude scrub depending on elevation band.

Wildlife notes for Minkiani Pass Trek: Himalayan birds (monal, griffons), langurs in forests, and occasional goral on steep grass — observe from distance.

Photography spots: Minkiani Pass main viewpoint; Valley light at golden hour; Camp ridgeline panoramas; Village / temple cultural frames.

Camping locations: Designated Minkiani Pass camping zones; Backup lower camp in bad weather.

Water sources: Carry adequate water from the last village; treat natural sources before drinking.

Network availability: Expect patchy or zero network beyond the last town. BSNL sometimes works in remote valleys.

Electricity availability: No reliable charging on trail — carry power banks.

faqs.question: How do I reach the trailhead for Lam Dal Trek?

faqs.question: What should I pack for Lam Dal Trek?

faqs.question: Is Lam Dal Trek safe for solo travellers?

faqs.question: What is the cancellation and refund policy?

faqs.question: What medical facilities are near Lam Dal Trek?

faqs.question: Is Lam Dal Trek crowded?

title: Toral Pass Trek
summary: Remote Toral Pass line across the Dhauladhar wilderness — for self-sufficient groups seeking solitude beyond popular Indrahar. From ₹16,999.
overview:
Toral Pass Trek is part of India Holiday Destinations’ Dharamshala and Dhauladhar collection. Remote Toral Pass line across the Dhauladhar wilderness — for self-sufficient groups seeking solitude beyond popular Indrahar.

Toral Pass is for those who have outgrown Indrahar crowds — remoter approaches, thinner trails, and true Dhauladhar solitude.

The itinerary usually starts at Chamba / Kangra remote approaches and ends at Toral Pass crossing, spanning about 52 km across 6 day(s) with a high point near 14,500 ft. Graded challenging, it demands respect for Himachal weather, Leave No Trace camping, and honest self-assessment before you book.

Best time to visit: April–June and September–November for most stable Dhauladhar weather

Flora along the Toral Pass corridor: Regional mix of chir pine, ban oak, rhododendron, deodar, and high-altitude scrub depending on elevation band.

Wildlife notes for Toral Pass Trek: Himalayan birds (monal, griffons), langurs in forests, and occasional goral on steep grass — observe from distance.

Photography spots: Toral Pass main viewpoint; Valley light at golden hour; Camp ridgeline panoramas; Village / temple cultural frames.

Camping locations: Designated Toral Pass camping zones; Backup lower camp in bad weather.

Water sources: Carry adequate water from the last village; treat natural sources before drinking.

Network availability: Expect patchy or zero network beyond the last town. BSNL sometimes works in remote valleys.

Electricity availability: No reliable charging on trail — carry power banks.

ATM availability: Last reliable ATMs in Dharamshala, McLeod Ganj, Palampur, Baijnath, or Bharmour depending on approach.

Medical facilities: Basic trail first aid; nearest substantial care in Dharamshala / regional district hospitals.

Permits required: Inner forest / camping permits as required for the specific corridor; included when stated in package.

Forest permissions: Follow Himachal Forest Department and local administration rules for camping, fire, and waste.

Safety on Toral Pass: Stay on marked trails and follow your trek leader’s briefings. Turn back early if weather deteriorates — Dhauladhar storms build quickly. Never wander alone after dark near cliffs, snow patches, or stream crossings. Always follow your trek leader’s brief for this specific route.

Travel tips: Arrive in Dhauladhar a night early when mornings start at the trailhead. Download offline maps; do not rely on trail network. Carry cash for village tea shops and emergency transport.

Responsible tourism on Toral Pass Trek: Leave no trace — pack out all non-biodegradable waste. Use designated camping zones and toilet practices advised by your guide. Support local porters, mules, and homestays fairly.

Nearby attractions from this trailhead: McLeod Ganj, Bhagsu Nag, Kangra Fort, Bir Billing, Masroor Temples.

Nearby treks: Waru Pass Trek, Snowline Trek, Laka Glacier Trek, Snowline Laka Trek.

Child policy for Toral Pass Trek: 16+ experienced preferred. Children are welcome on easier Dhauladhar trails when accompanied by a parent or guardian. Minimum age depends on the route (typically 8+ for short day hikes, 12+ for overnight alpine camps). Share ages at booking for route-specific advice.

Private departures: Private Toral Pass Trek departures can be arranged for families and corporate groups subject to guide availability.

Group discount: Group discount available for 6+ travellers on the same fixed departure.

Booking and cancellation policies for India Holiday Destinations packages apply — see FAQs on this page for the current fee schedule.

Things to carry beyond the packing list: Layered clothing; Rain shell; Headlamp; Personal medicines; Reusable water bottle.

Things not to carry: Single-use plastics beyond reusable bottles; Heavy jeans or cotton-heavy clothing for multi-day wet weather; Unnecessary valuables and excess jewellery; Alcohol and recreational drugs.

overview:
Galu Devi Trek is part of India Holiday Destinations’ Dharamshala and Dhauladhar collection. Popular short climb from Dharamkot to Galu Devi — prayer flags, café culture, and the classic start toward Triund.

Galu Devi is Dharamkot’s classic prayer-flag climb and the cultural doorstep of the Triund trail system.

The itinerary usually starts at Dharamkot and ends at Galu Devi Temple, spanning about 4 km across 1 day(s) with a high point near 7,000 ft. Graded easy, it demands respect for Himachal weather, Leave No Trace camping, and honest self-assessment before you book.

Best time to visit: April–June and September–November for most stable Dhauladhar weather

Flora along the Galu Devi corridor: Regional mix of chir pine, ban oak, rhododendron, deodar, and high-altitude scrub depending on elevation band.

Wildlife notes for Galu Devi Trek: Himalayan birds (monal, griffons), langurs in forests, and occasional goral on steep grass — observe from distance.

Photography spots: Galu Devi main viewpoint; Valley light at golden hour; Camp ridgeline panoramas; Village / temple cultural frames.

Camping locations: Not applicable — day trek.

Water sources: Carry adequate water from the last village; treat natural sources before drinking.

Network availability: Expect patchy or zero network beyond the last town. BSNL sometimes works in remote valleys.

Electricity availability: Charge devices in town before starting.

ATM availability: Last reliable ATMs in Dharamshala, McLeod Ganj, Palampur, Baijnath, or Bharmour depending on approach.

Medical facilities: Basic trail first aid; nearest substantial care in Dharamshala / regional district hospitals.

Permits required: Inner forest / camping permits as required for the specific corridor; included when stated in package.

Forest permissions: Follow Himachal Forest Department and local administration rules for camping, fire, and waste.

Safety on Galu Devi: Stay on marked trails and follow your trek leader’s briefings. Turn back early if weather deteriorates — Dhauladhar storms build quickly. Never wander alone after dark near cliffs, snow patches, or stream crossings. Always follow your trek leader’s brief for this specific route.

Travel tips: Arrive in Dharamkot a night early when mornings start at the trailhead. Download offline maps; do not rely on trail network. Carry cash for village tea shops and emergency transport.

Responsible tourism on Galu Devi Trek: Leave no trace — pack out all non-biodegradable waste. Use designated camping zones and toilet practices advised by your guide. Support local porters, mules, and homestays fairly.

Nearby attractions from this trailhead: McLeod Ganj, Bhagsu Nag, Kangra Fort, Bir Billing, Masroor Temples.

Nearby treks: Bagalu Nag Trek, Kareri to Triund Traverse, Toral Pass Trek, Snowline Laka Trek.

Child policy for Galu Devi Trek: 8+ with guardians. Children are welcome on easier Dhauladhar trails when accompanied by a parent or guardian. Minimum age depends on the route (typically 8+ for short day hikes, 12+ for overnight alpine camps). Share ages at booking for route-specific advice.

Group discount: Group discount available for 6+ travellers on the same fixed departure.

faqs.answer: Basic clinics exist in Dharamkot. For serious emergencies, evacuation aims for Civil / Zonal Hospital Dharamshala. Carry personal medication and inform us of any conditions before departure.

faqs.answer: Popular sections near towns can be busy on weekends; early starts help.

faqs.question: Can I combine Galu Devi with Triund?
faqs.answer: Many travellers pair easier Dharamshala walks with Triund or Kareri Lake on separate days. Ask us for a custom multi-trek plan.

title: Kunal Pathri Trek
summary: Sacred hill walk to Kunal Pathri Temple — pine forests, local pilgrims, and a serene half-day outing from Dharamshala. From ₹799.
overview:
Kunal Pathri Trek is part of India Holiday Destinations’ Dharamshala and Dhauladhar collection. Sacred hill walk to Kunal Pathri Temple — pine forests, local pilgrims, and a serene half-day outing from Dharamshala.

Kunal Pathri’s temple hill is pine-scented and locally loved — a half-day cultural climb when you want calm over conquest.

The itinerary usually starts at Kunal Pathri Temple approach and ends at Kunal Pathri, spanning about 5 km across 1 day(s) with a high point near 6,500 ft. Graded easy, it demands respect for Himachal weather, Leave No Trace camping, and honest self-assessment before you book.

title: Snowline Trek
summary: 3D/2N from Bhagsunag — Triund camp, then climb to Laka Glacier / Snowline with Indrahar Pass views. From ₹2,500.
overview:
Snowline Laka (also called Snowline or Laka Got) sits in the Dhauladhar range above Triund near McLeod Ganj. If Triund is on your list, this extension takes you closer to the snowline and the approach toward Indrahar Pass.

The trail is steeper beyond Triund and rewards you with glacier scenery, possible snow walks in season, and occasional wildlife sightings such as mountain goats. Camping nights at Triund and Laka make it a fuller weekend than a single Triund overnight.

Rated easy to moderate, the 3 days / 2 nights itinerary starts at Bhagsunag at 10:00 AM and typically finishes back there around 2:00 PM on Day 3.

itinerary.title: Bhagsunag to Triund Top
itinerary.description:
Meet at 10:00 AM at Bhagsunag Temple — the trek start. Hike toward Triund via the Bhagsu Falls route. Pause 10–20 minutes at the waterfall, then continue to the Leta midpoint to rest and eat.

Reach Triund Top campsite around 3:00 PM for ridge views of the Dhauladhars and Kangra Valley. Enjoy sunset colours, dinner, and an overnight camp at Triund.

itinerary.title: Triund to Snowline / Laka Glacier
itinerary.description:
Wake for sunrise and breakfast around 8:00 AM. Continue from Triund Top toward Laka Glacier / Snowline — about 6 km and usually 2–3 hours. The trail often becomes snowier as you gain height (~3,200 m).

At Laka, take in Indrahar Pass views up close. Evening bonfire and dinner around 8:00 PM (when conditions allow). Overnight at Laka Camp.

itinerary.title: Laka to Bhagsunag
itinerary.description: After breakfast, descend roughly 12 km from Laka Got / Snowline all the way to Bhagsunag. The trek typically ends around 2:00 PM — time enough for Bhagsunag Temple, café stops in McLeod Ganj, or onward travel.

faqs.question: Is Snowline Laka Trek difficult?
faqs.answer: It is rated easy to moderate. Day 1 follows the classic Triund trail; Day 2 adds a steeper, rockier climb to Laka / Snowline (~3,200 m). Beginners with basic fitness manage it when paced with a guide.

faqs.question: Will there be snow on the Snowline / Laka trail?
faqs.answer: Snow cover increases in winter and early spring. From late autumn through March you are more likely to walk on snow patches or continuous snow near Laka Glacier. Summer may show only patches depending on the season.

faqs.question: How is this trek connected to Triund?
faqs.answer: Day 1 camps at Triund Top after the Bhagsunag–waterfall–Leta climb. Day 2 continues ~6 km beyond Triund to Laka Glacier / Snowline with views toward Indrahar Pass, then Day 3 descends all the way to Bhagsunag.

faqs.question: Where does the Snowline Laka Trek start?
faqs.answer: Meet at Bhagsunag (Bhagsu Nag), Dharamshala, by 10:00 AM on Day 1 near the temple / taxi stand area. The route goes via Bhagsu waterfall toward Triund, then onward to Laka.

faqs.question: How long is the Snowline Laka Trek?
faqs.answer: Plan for 3 days / 2 nights. Total walking is around 24 km including Triund and the Laka extension. Day 3 typically ends back at Bhagsunag around 2:00 PM.

faqs.question: What is the cancellation and refund policy?

faqs.question: What medical facilities are near Laka Glacier Trek?

faqs.question: Is Laka Glacier Trek crowded?

title: Kheerganga Trek
summary: 2D/1N from Barshaini — Parvati Valley forests, waterfalls, camping, and a natural hot spring at the top. From ₹1,099.
overview:
Kheerganga is one of Himachal’s most loved weekend treks, tucked deep in the Parvati Valley near Kasol. People come for the hot springs at Parvati Kund and for wide views over a valley of pine, oak, and apple orchards.

The walk is about 12 km one way (roughly 24 km both ways) on a mostly gentle trail that follows the Parvati River past waterfalls and small villages. Some stretches steepen, but the overall grade suits beginners who can manage 5–7 hours of walking with a light pack.

After the climb, safari-style camping and a dip in the mineral hot springs make a classic Kasol weekend. Pair it with Tosh or Kasol cafés if you have an extra half day.

quickInfo.groupSize: Join a group | Weekend departures
quickInfo.accommodation: Safari camps (triple / quad sharing, common washroom)
quickInfo.transport: Self arrange to Barshaini via Kasol

highlights: Hot spring bath at Parvati Kund after the ascent
Scenic Parvati Valley villages and lush forest trails
Snow-capped mountain views from the top on clear days
Narrow trail along the river with waterfall crossings
Evening bonfire with music (when conditions allow)
Guided group trek with safari camping

itinerary.title: Barshaini to Kheerganga
itinerary.description:
Reach Barshaini — the start of the Kheerganga trek (~3,050 m). Please arrive by 10:00 AM. Stock snacks or water in the village before you leave.

The trail begins as a gentle climb past apple orchards, then moves into deodar and oak forest with the Parvati River roaring below. Expect about 12 km and 5–6 hours of walking, with waterfall and village breaks along the way.

On arrival, settle into safari tents, then soak at Parvati Kund (natural hot springs) — especially rewarding in colder months. Evening tea, dinner, and a group bonfire with music (typically till 11:00 PM when permitted) wrap up the day.

itinerary.title: Kheerganga to Barshaini
itinerary.description:
Start with morning tea in the quiet of Parvati Valley. After breakfast, descend the same ~12 km trail back to Barshaini in about 4–5 hours, typically finishing by around 2:00 PM.

Pause in the villages and forest clearings for photos on the way down. Once at Barshaini, you are free to explore nearby Tosh or return to Kasol for cafés, Manikaran, or onward travel.

inclusions: Day 1 — Evening tea and dinner (dal, sabji, chapati, rice)
Day 2 — Breakfast (veg parantha / poori bhaji with tea)
Evening group bonfire with music till ~11:00 PM (when available)
Experienced group guide from Barshaini to Kheerganga and back
Stay in safari camps (triple / quad sharing) with common washroom
exclusions: Any cost arising due to natural calamities
Transport to / from Kasol or Barshaini
Hot spring entry fees (if charged locally)
Any other service not mentioned in inclusions

fitness.description: Kheerganga suits beginners with basic fitness. Expect 5–7 hours of walking on Day 1 with sustained but mostly moderate climbs. You should be comfortable on forest trails with a daypack.
tips: Walk 3–4 km daily for two weeks before departure
Practice stair climbs with a light backpack
Hydrate well and avoid alcohol the night before
Break in your trek shoes beforehand
Arrive at Barshaini by 10:00 AM on Day 1

map.overview: Start and end at Barshaini (Bharshaini), Parvati Valley near Kasol. Day 1 climbs ~12 km through orchards and forest to Kheerganga (~3,050 m). Day 2 descends the same route. Reach Kasol via Bhuntar (bus/air) or Pathankot (rail), then transfer to Barshaini (~45 minutes from Kasol).
map.elevationNote: Max altitude ~3,050 m (10,006 ft). Modest incline with steeper mid sections.

weather.note: Cold; snow possible

faqs.question: Will we be able to find snow in Kheerganga?
faqs.answer: Yes, you can often see snow from late November through March. Snow is not guaranteed year-round. Winter nights stay cold. For reliable snow views in the wider region, Kasol, Tosh, and Manali are popular nearby stops.

faqs.question: Can inexperienced trekkers hike the Kheerganga trail?
faqs.answer: Yes. Kheerganga is a great first Himalayan trek for people with ordinary fitness and no prior experience. Families regularly do it; parents or guardians must accompany children under 15.

faqs.question: Is Kheerganga Trek difficult?
faqs.answer: It is rated easy to moderate. The one-way distance is about 12 km with waterfall crossings and some steeper stretches. Most people take 4–6 hours uphill. The trail is largely a forest and village walk that beginners complete successfully.

faqs.question: Is there mobile network in Kheerganga?
faqs.answer: Coverage on the trail and at the top is poor. BSNL works in patches; other networks often fail. Make important calls from Kasol or Barshaini before you start.

faqs.question: What are the risks of high-altitude trekking?
faqs.answer: Altitude can cause headache, nausea, or fatigue. Tell your trek leader if you feel unwell and carry personal medication. The team carries a basic first-aid kit; rest, hydrate, and descend if symptoms worsen.

faqs.question: What are the most important Kheerganga trekking tips?
faqs.answer: Prepare for changing weather, carry personal medicine, go with a guide if you are new to hiking, expect remote camping with limited amenities, avoid wasting food or water, follow camp rules, and stay with your group.

faqs.question: Is it advisable to take the Kheerganga trek in winter?
faqs.answer: The route stays open in winter, but snow can make sections slippery and slow. Use proper trekking shoes and poles, and prefer a guided group. The hot springs at the top feel especially rewarding after a cold climb.

faqs.question: What is Kheerganga well-known for?
faqs.answer: Kheerganga is known for natural hot springs (Parvati Kund), camping under Himalayan skies, forest trails through Parvati Valley villages, and a relaxed weekend escape from city life — often paired with time in Kasol.

faqs.question: Can we spend the night in Kheerganga?
faqs.answer: Overnight camping is arranged as part of this 2 days / 1 night package when permitted by local guidelines. After a 5–7 hour ascent, you camp near the hot springs and return the next day.

faqs.question: Where does the Kheerganga Trek begin?
faqs.answer: The trek starts at Barshaini (also spelled Bharshaini), near the Parvati dam, at roughly 1,189 m. It is a short drive from Kasol and about three hours by road from Manali. Overnight camping is at Kheerganga (~3,050 m).

faqs.question: What is the history behind the Kheerganga name?
faqs.answer: Local lore links the area with Lord Shiva and Kartikeya. “Kheerganga” refers to the milky-white sacred hot spring water. A dip in Parvati Kund is considered holy, and there is an old temple near the springs.

faqs.question: Is drinking water available on the Kheerganga trail?
faqs.answer: Carry what you need from Barshaini, as shops thin out after the start. You can refill at natural springs and ashrams along the route when available — ask your guide which sources are safe.

faqs.question: How do I get to Kheerganga / Barshaini?
faqs.answer: There is no direct rail or road from Delhi to Kasol. By road: overnight bus from Delhi ISBT Kashmere Gate / Majnu-Ka-Tila toward Kullu–Manali, alight at Bhuntar, then local bus to Kasol and cab or shared jeep to Barshaini. By rail: Pathankot or Joginder Nagar, then road to Kasol. By air: Bhuntar (Kullu) Airport, about 31 km from Kasol, then onward to Barshaini. Arrive by 10:00 AM on Day 1.

faqs.question: What places can I visit near Kheerganga?
faqs.answer: Tosh and Barshaini are scenic village stops. Kasol cafés and riverside walks are popular, Manikaran Sahib is a short hop away, and Manali is about three hours by road. Chalal and Malana make good add-on day hikes if you have extra time.

faqs.question: What is the best time to visit Kheerganga?
faqs.answer: March to June is the most popular window — pleasant days and clearer views. September to November is also excellent after the rains. Monsoon is lush but slipperier; winter brings cold and possible snow.

faqs.question: What is included in the trek fee?
faqs.answer: Day 1 evening tea and dinner, Day 2 breakfast, evening group bonfire with music (till about 11:00 PM when conditions allow), experienced guide Barshaini–Kheerganga–Barshaini, and safari camps on a triple/quad sharing basis with common washroom.

title: Kugti Pass Trek
summary: Classic Kugti Pass traverse from the sacred Kugti valley — temples, high pastures, and a demanding alpine crossing. From ₹24,999.
overview:
Kugti Pass Trek is part of India Holiday Destinations’ Dharamshala and Dhauladhar collection. Classic Kugti Pass traverse from the sacred Kugti valley — temples, high pastures, and a demanding alpine crossing.

Kugti Pass rises from a sacred valley of temples and herding culture into a stern alpine gate toward Lahaul — long, beautiful, and demanding.

The itinerary usually starts at Kugti Village and ends at Lahaul / return options, spanning about 60 km across 7 day(s) with a high point near 16,500 ft. Graded challenging, it demands respect for Himachal weather, Leave No Trace camping, and honest self-assessment before you book.

Best time to visit: April–June and September–November for most stable Dhauladhar weather

Flora along the Kugti Pass corridor: Regional mix of chir pine, ban oak, rhododendron, deodar, and high-altitude scrub depending on elevation band.

Wildlife notes for Kugti Pass Trek: Himalayan birds (monal, griffons), langurs in forests, and occasional goral on steep grass — observe from distance.

Photography spots: Kugti Pass main viewpoint; Valley light at golden hour; Camp ridgeline panoramas; Village / temple cultural frames.

Camping locations: Designated Kugti Pass camping zones; Backup lower camp in bad weather.

Water sources: Carry adequate water from the last village; treat natural sources before drinking.

Network availability: Expect patchy or zero network beyond the last town. BSNL sometimes works in remote valleys.

Electricity availability: No reliable charging on trail — carry power banks.

ATM availability: Last reliable ATMs in Dharamshala, McLeod Ganj, Palampur, Baijnath, or Bharmour depending on approach.

Medical facilities: Basic trail first aid; nearest substantial care in Dharamshala / regional district hospitals.

Permits required: Inner forest / camping permits as required for the specific corridor; included when stated in package.

Forest permissions: Follow Himachal Forest Department and local administration rules for camping, fire, and waste.

Safety on Kugti Pass: Stay on marked trails and follow your trek leader’s briefings. Turn back early if weather deteriorates — Dhauladhar storms build quickly. Never wander alone after dark near cliffs, snow patches, or stream crossings. Always follow your trek leader’s brief for this specific route.

Travel tips: Arrive in Bharmour a night early when mornings start at the trailhead. Download offline maps; do not rely on trail network. Carry cash for village tea shops and emergency transport.

Responsible tourism on Kugti Pass Trek: Leave no trace — pack out all non-biodegradable waste. Use designated camping zones and toilet practices advised by your guide. Support local porters, mules, and homestays fairly.

Nearby attractions from this trailhead: McLeod Ganj, Bhagsu Nag, Kangra Fort, Bir Billing, Masroor Temples.

Nearby treks: Jalsu Pass Trek, Thamsar Pass Trek, Rajgundha Trek, Triund Trek.

Child policy for Kugti Pass Trek: 16+ experienced preferred. Children are welcome on easier Dhauladhar trails when accompanied by a parent or guardian. Minimum age depends on the route (typically 8+ for short day hikes, 12+ for overnight alpine camps). Share ages at booking for route-specific advice.

Private departures: Private Kugti Pass Trek departures can be arranged for families and corporate groups subject to guide availability.

Group discount: Group discount available for 6+ travellers on the same fixed departure.

Booking and cancellation policies for India Holiday Destinations packages apply — see FAQs on this page for the current fee schedule.

Things to carry beyond the packing list: Layered clothing; Rain shell; Headlamp; Personal medicines; Reusable water bottle.

Things not to carry: Single-use plastics beyond reusable bottles; Heavy jeans or cotton-heavy clothing for multi-day wet weather; Unnecessary valuables and excess jewellery; Alcohol and recreational drugs.

faqs.question: What is the difficulty level of the Kugti Pass Trek?
faqs.answer: This route is rated challenging. The Kugti Pass Trek demands challenging effort across 7 day(s). Train with stair climbs and loaded walks; respect altitude above 16500 ft.

faqs.question: How long does the Kugti Pass Trek take?
faqs.answer: The standard package is 7D / 6N. Actual walking time varies with fitness, weather, and trail conditions.

faqs.question: What is the total trekking distance for Kugti Pass Trek?
faqs.answer: Expect roughly 60 km of walking across the itinerary (out-and-back or point-to-point depending on the route plan).

faqs.question: What is the maximum altitude on Kugti Pass Trek?
faqs.answer: The highest point is about 16,500 ft (5,029 m). Ascend steadily, hydrate, and tell your guide if you feel altitude-related symptoms on higher Dhauladhar routes.

faqs.question: Where does the Kugti Pass Trek start?
faqs.answer: The usual starting point is Kugti Village. Arrive the evening before when the itinerary begins early.

faqs.question: When is the best time for Kugti Pass Trek?
faqs.answer: April–June and September–November for most stable Dhauladhar weather

faqs.question: Is mobile network available on Kugti Pass Trek?
faqs.answer: Expect patchy or zero network beyond the last town. BSNL sometimes works in remote valleys.

faqs.question: Do I need permits for Kugti Pass Trek?
faqs.answer: Inner forest / camping permits as required for the specific corridor; included when stated in package.

faqs.question: Is drinking water available on the Kugti Pass Trek trail?
faqs.answer: Carry adequate water from the last village; treat natural sources before drinking.

faqs.question: Where do we camp or stay on Kugti Pass Trek?
faqs.answer: Designated Kugti Pass camping zones Backup lower camp in bad weather

faqs.question: Can beginners attempt the Kugti Pass Trek?
faqs.answer: It depends on the grade. The Kugti Pass Trek demands challenging effort across 7 day(s). Train with stair climbs and loaded walks; respect altitude above 16500 ft. If you are new to the Himalayas, choose a guided departure and prepare with regular walks carrying a daypack.

faqs.question: How do I reach the trailhead for Kugti Pass Trek?
faqs.answer: Fly into Gaggal (DHM) or take a train to Pathankot, then road transfer to Bharmour. Local taxis and shared jeeps cover the final approach to Kugti Village.

faqs.question: What should I pack for Kugti Pass Trek?
faqs.answer: Carry layered clothing, broken-in trekking shoes, rain shell, headlamp, sunscreen, personal medicines, and a power bank. A detailed packing list is provided on this page and with your booking confirmation.

faqs.question: Is Kugti Pass Trek safe for solo travellers?
faqs.answer: Guided group departures are the safest option for solo travellers in the Dhauladhar. Trails can be remote, weather changes fast, and night navigation is risky without local knowledge.

faqs.question: What is the cancellation and refund policy?
faqs.answer: Cancellations 45+ days before departure: 25% fee. 15–30 days: 50% fee. Within 0–15 days: 100% of the trek cost. For weather or government restrictions, we try to offer a viable alternative; cash refund may not be available. Approved refunds are credited to the original payment method in 10–12 working days.

faqs.question: Are meals included on the Kugti Pass Trek?
faqs.answer: Trek-day meals are included as listed in the package inclusions (typically vegetarian). Confirm dietary needs when you book so the kitchen team can plan.

faqs.question: What medical facilities are near Kugti Pass Trek?
faqs.answer: Basic clinics exist in Bharmour. For serious emergencies, evacuation aims for Civil / Zonal Hospital Dharamshala. Carry personal medication and inform us of any conditions before departure.

faqs.question: Is Kugti Pass Trek crowded?
faqs.answer: Kugti Pass sees far fewer groups than Triund — expect quieter camps and longer self-sufficiency.

faqs.question: Can I combine Kugti Pass with Triund?
faqs.answer: Many travellers pair easier Dharamshala walks with Triund or Kareri Lake on separate days. Ask us for a custom multi-trek plan.

title: Hanuman Tibba Base Camp Trek
summary: Expedition-style approach toward Hanuman Ka Tibba (Friendship Peak massif region logistics vary) with high camps, glacier travel briefing, and serious mountain days. From ₹24,999.
overview:
Hanuman Ka Tibba Base Camp is part of India Holiday Destinations’ Dharamshala and Dhauladhar collection. Expedition-style approach toward Hanuman Ka Tibba (Friendship Peak massif region logistics vary) with high camps, glacier travel briefing, and serious mountain days.

Base-camp logistics for Hanuman Ka Tibba demand expedition discipline — staged camps, glacier briefings, and weather patience before any summit talk begins.

The itinerary usually starts at Bharmour / designated roadhead and ends at Base camp below Hanuman Tibba approaches, spanning about 55 km across 6 day(s) with a high point near 15,500 ft. Graded challenging, it demands respect for Himachal weather, Leave No Trace camping, and honest self-assessment before you book.

Best time to visit: April–June and September–November for most stable Dhauladhar weather

Flora along the Hanuman Ka Tibba BC corridor: Regional mix of chir pine, ban oak, rhododendron, deodar, and high-altitude scrub depending on elevation band.

Wildlife notes for Hanuman Ka Tibba Base Camp: Himalayan birds (monal, griffons), langurs in forests, and occasional goral on steep grass — observe from distance.

Photography spots: Hanuman Ka Tibba BC main viewpoint; Valley light at golden hour; Camp ridgeline panoramas; Village / temple cultural frames.

Camping locations: Designated Hanuman Ka Tibba BC camping zones; Backup lower camp in bad weather.

Water sources: Carry adequate water from the last village; treat natural sources before drinking.

Network availability: Expect patchy or zero network beyond the last town. BSNL sometimes works in remote valleys.

Electricity availability: No reliable charging on trail — carry power banks.

ATM availability: Last reliable ATMs in Dharamshala, McLeod Ganj, Palampur, Baijnath, or Bharmour depending on approach.

Medical facilities: Basic trail first aid; nearest substantial care in Dharamshala / regional district hospitals.

Permits required: Inner forest / camping permits as required for the specific corridor; included when stated in package.

Forest permissions: Follow Himachal Forest Department and local administration rules for camping, fire, and waste.

Safety on Hanuman Ka Tibba BC: Stay on marked trails and follow your trek leader’s briefings. Turn back early if weather deteriorates — Dhauladhar storms build quickly. Never wander alone after dark near cliffs, snow patches, or stream crossings. Always follow your trek leader’s brief for this specific route.

Travel tips: Arrive in Dharamshala / Bharmour approach a night early when mornings start at the trailhead. Download offline maps; do not rely on trail network. Carry cash for village tea shops and emergency transport.

Responsible tourism on Hanuman Ka Tibba Base Camp: Leave no trace — pack out all non-biodegradable waste. Use designated camping zones and toilet practices advised by your guide. Support local porters, mules, and homestays fairly.

Nearby attractions from this trailhead: McLeod Ganj, Bhagsu Nag, Kangra Fort, Bir Billing, Masroor Temples.

Nearby treks: Hanuman Ka Tibba Expedition, Moon Peak Trek, Dhauladhar Base Camp Trek, Kareri Lake Trek.

Child policy for Hanuman Ka Tibba Base Camp: 16+ experienced preferred. Children are welcome on easier Dhauladhar trails when accompanied by a parent or guardian. Minimum age depends on the route (typically 8+ for short day hikes, 12+ for overnight alpine camps). Share ages at booking for route-specific advice.

Private departures: Private Hanuman Ka Tibba Base Camp departures can be arranged for families and corporate groups subject to guide availability.

Group discount: Group discount available for 6+ travellers on the same fixed departure.

Booking and cancellation policies for India Holiday Destinations packages apply — see FAQs on this page for the current fee schedule.

Things to carry beyond the packing list: Layered clothing; Rain shell; Headlamp; Personal medicines; Reusable water bottle.

Things not to carry: Single-use plastics beyond reusable bottles; Heavy jeans or cotton-heavy clothing for multi-day wet weather; Unnecessary valuables and excess jewellery; Alcohol and recreational drugs.

faqs.question: What is the difficulty level of the Hanuman Ka Tibba Base Camp?

faqs.question: How long does the Hanuman Ka Tibba Base Camp take?
faqs.answer: The standard package is 6D / 5N. Actual walking time varies with fitness, weather, and trail conditions.

faqs.question: What is the total trekking distance for Hanuman Ka Tibba Base Camp?
faqs.answer: Expect roughly 55 km of walking across the itinerary (out-and-back or point-to-point depending on the route plan).

faqs.question: What is the maximum altitude on Hanuman Ka Tibba Base Camp?
faqs.answer: The highest point is about 15,500 ft (4,724 m). Ascend steadily, hydrate, and tell your guide if you feel altitude-related symptoms on higher Dhauladhar routes.

faqs.question: Can beginners attempt the Hanuman Ka Tibba Base Camp?
faqs.answer: It depends on the grade. The Hanuman Ka Tibba Base Camp demands challenging effort across 6 day(s). Train with stair climbs and loaded walks; respect altitude above 15500 ft. If you are new to the Himalayas, choose a guided departure and prepare with regular walks carrying a daypack.

faqs.question: How do I reach the trailhead for Hanuman Ka Tibba Base Camp?
faqs.answer: Fly into Gaggal (DHM) or take a train to Pathankot, then road transfer to Dharamshala / Bharmour approach. Local taxis and shared jeeps cover the final approach to Bharmour / designated roadhead.

faqs.question: What should I pack for Hanuman Ka Tibba Base Camp?
faqs.answer: Carry layered clothing, broken-in trekking shoes, rain shell, headlamp, sunscreen, personal medicines, and a power bank. A detailed packing list is provided on this page and with your booking confirmation.

faqs.question: Is Hanuman Ka Tibba Base Camp safe for solo travellers?
faqs.answer: Guided group departures are the safest option for solo travellers in the Dhauladhar. Trails can be remote, weather changes fast, and night navigation is risky without local knowledge.

faqs.question: What is the cancellation and refund policy?
faqs.answer: Cancellations 45+ days before departure: 25% fee. 15–30 days: 50% fee. Within 0–15 days: 100% of the trek cost. For weather or government restrictions, we try to offer a viable alternative; cash refund may not be available. Approved refunds are credited to the original payment method in 10–12 working days.

faqs.question: Are meals included on the Hanuman Ka Tibba Base Camp?
faqs.answer: Trek-day meals are included as listed in the package inclusions (typically vegetarian). Confirm dietary needs when you book so the kitchen team can plan.

faqs.question: What medical facilities are near Hanuman Ka Tibba Base Camp?
faqs.answer: Basic clinics exist in Dharamshala / Bharmour approach. For serious emergencies, evacuation aims for Civil / Zonal Hospital Dharamshala. Carry personal medication and inform us of any conditions before departure.

faqs.question: Is Hanuman Ka Tibba Base Camp crowded?
faqs.answer: Hanuman Ka Tibba BC sees far fewer groups than Triund — expect quieter camps and longer self-sufficiency.

faqs.question: Can I combine Hanuman Ka Tibba BC with Triund?
faqs.answer: Many travellers pair easier Dharamshala walks with Triund or Kareri Lake on separate days. Ask us for a custom multi-trek plan.

faqs.question: What is the difficulty level of the Bara Bhangal Trek?
faqs.answer: This route is rated difficult. The Bara Bhangal Trek demands difficult effort across 10 day(s). Train with stair climbs and loaded walks; respect altitude above 15000 ft.

faqs.question: How long does the Bara Bhangal Trek take?
faqs.answer: The standard package is 10D / 9N. Actual walking time varies with fitness, weather, and trail conditions.

faqs.question: What is the total trekking distance for Bara Bhangal Trek?
faqs.answer: Expect roughly 95 km of walking across the itinerary (out-and-back or point-to-point depending on the route plan).

faqs.question: What is the maximum altitude on Bara Bhangal Trek?
faqs.answer: The highest point is about 15,000 ft (4,572 m). Ascend steadily, hydrate, and tell your guide if you feel altitude-related symptoms on higher Dhauladhar routes.

faqs.question: Where does the Bara Bhangal Trek start?
faqs.answer: The usual starting point is Billing–Rajgundha or Mandi approaches. Arrive the evening before when the itinerary begins early.

faqs.question: When is the best time for Bara Bhangal Trek?
faqs.answer: July–September weather windows only; avoid deep winter

faqs.question: Is mobile network available on Bara Bhangal Trek?
faqs.answer: Expect patchy or zero network beyond the last town. BSNL sometimes works in remote valleys.

faqs.question: Do I need permits for Bara Bhangal Trek?
faqs.answer: Inner forest / camping permits as required for the specific corridor; included when stated in package.

faqs.question: Is drinking water available on the Bara Bhangal Trek trail?
faqs.answer: Carry adequate water from the last village; treat natural sources before drinking.

faqs.question: Where do we camp or stay on Bara Bhangal Trek?
faqs.answer: Designated Bara Bhangal camping zones Backup lower camp in bad weather

faqs.question: Can beginners attempt the Bara Bhangal Trek?
faqs.answer: It depends on the grade. The Bara Bhangal Trek demands difficult effort across 10 day(s). Train with stair climbs and loaded walks; respect altitude above 15000 ft. If you are new to the Himalayas, choose a guided departure and prepare with regular walks carrying a daypack.

faqs.question: How do I reach the trailhead for Bara Bhangal Trek?
faqs.answer: Fly into Gaggal (DHM) or take a train to Pathankot, then road transfer to Bara Bhangal. Local taxis and shared jeeps cover the final approach to Billing–Rajgundha or Mandi approaches.

faqs.question: What should I pack for Bara Bhangal Trek?
faqs.answer: Carry layered clothing, broken-in trekking shoes, rain shell, headlamp, sunscreen, personal medicines, and a power bank. A detailed packing list is provided on this page and with your booking confirmation.

faqs.question: Is Bara Bhangal Trek safe for solo travellers?
faqs.answer: Guided group departures are the safest option for solo travellers in the Dhauladhar. Trails can be remote, weather changes fast, and night navigation is risky without local knowledge.

faqs.question: What is the cancellation and refund policy?
faqs.answer: Cancellations 45+ days before departure: 25% fee. 15–30 days: 50% fee. Within 0–15 days: 100% of the trek cost. For weather or government restrictions, we try to offer a viable alternative; cash refund may not be available. Approved refunds are credited to the original payment method in 10–12 working days.

faqs.question: Are meals included on the Bara Bhangal Trek?
faqs.answer: Trek-day meals are included as listed in the package inclusions (typically vegetarian). Confirm dietary needs when you book so the kitchen team can plan.

faqs.question: What medical facilities are near Bara Bhangal Trek?
faqs.answer: Basic clinics exist in Bara Bhangal. For serious emergencies, evacuation aims for Civil / Zonal Hospital Dharamshala. Carry personal medication and inform us of any conditions before departure.

faqs.question: Is Bara Bhangal Trek crowded?
faqs.answer: Bara Bhangal sees far fewer groups than Triund — expect quieter camps and longer self-sufficiency.

faqs.question: Can I combine Bara Bhangal with Triund?
faqs.answer: Many travellers pair easier Dharamshala walks with Triund or Kareri Lake on separate days. Ask us for a custom multi-trek plan.
